key_points: One student transferred out citing 'rich white snobby kids who don't even like to party'
Small school spirit is acknowledged even by committed students
DC location dominates social life more than campus itself
key_quotes: I went to a somewhat poor high school and now I am at a private university with a bunch of rich white snobby kids WHO DONT EVEN LIKE TO PARTY! I really just hate the social scene here
my anxiety and inability to be happy for myself is taking over. I keep thinking of all the things that are cons to me (far away from home, small school spirit, gender inequality)
I really enjoyed the school and decided I'm about ready to commit

key_points: No specific dining praise found in student data
High cost of attendance makes meal plan a financial burden
DC location means students have strong off-campus food options
key_quotes: Don't even have a meal plan so the restaurants really are a luxury

going to Washington DC and visiting all the cool museums

key_points: No AU-specific mental health resources mentioned by students
General college stress culture is a recurring theme across posts
Transfer students report feeling lost and unsupported at AU
key_quotes: I came into AU intending to major in Poli Sci but quickly realized I don't wanna go that route. Not wanting to major in that, combined with the fact that I didn't really have the best time at AU, made me decide to apply as a transfer

key_points: Presidential scholarship (25k/year) exists but still leaves significant costs
AU used as Georgetown safety — aware of this, affects RD admissions strategy
Merit aid exists but requires proactive research; not auto-awarded
key_quotes: they gave me the presidential scholarship (25k a year) plus a 10k grant. I would still have to pay 10k a year out of pocket compared to nothing at GMU
I got 15k per year in merit scholarships for American
AU Merit Aid — Has anyone received any merit aid from American and if so can you share your stats? Any info about their scholarships is also appreciated

key_points: STEM programs widely considered weak — multiple students flag this
SIS (School of International Service) has strong reputation for IR/policy
Honors and Lincoln Scholars programs offer meaningful academic upgrades

key_points: Portal reliability issues — students report it going down on decision days
Honors College application is a separate, easily-missed process
Spring admits report confusion and lack of guidance from administration
key_quotes: 1 hr and 24 minutes later, the AU portal is still down. Nice.
hate au cuz I just noticed that their honors college application is a whole separate application. I hate it here

key_points: DC location is the single biggest draw — internships, politics, culture
Campus itself described as traditional and smaller than GW's urban sprawl
AU vs GW campus feel is a key decision point for prospective students
key_quotes: AU has a smaller and more intimate campus similar to most traditional colleges while GW is more sprawled and urban
I LOVE it, the city is amazing and I can see myself living there forever
going to Washington DC has always been my favorite city

key_points: DC location provides unmatched internship access, especially in politics/IR
AU Cornerstone program guarantees DC internship credit as a freshman
Weak STEM reputation may limit career outcomes outside policy/IR fields
key_quotes: I got 15k per year in merit scholarships for American and was admitted to the AU Cornerstone program so I could do an internship in DC for class credit as a freshman
AU is technically better for the major I want [IR]
I want to major in Political Science and then attend law school and become a lawyer. Is paying the 10k worth cancelling my commitment to GMU and going to AU?

key_points: High sticker price with significant out-of-pocket cost even after scholarships
GMU (free) vs AU (10k/year) is a real dilemma students wrestle with publicly
Strong value IF you're in IR/policy; poor value for STEM majors
key_quotes: I'm a Senior from DC and have already committed to GMU but I got off AU waitlist... I would still have to pay 10k a year out of pocket compared to nothing at GMU
the cost of attendance is about half the price with reciprocity tuition [at UW-Madison vs AU]
AU honors vs GW — AU would cost about $10–13K less per year

key_points: AU is a DC politics school first — culture revolves around policy and IR

key_points: Antisemitism: federal complaint cites student spat on, swastikas on dorm doors, FBI protective detail assigned
Social scene: student transferred out citing hostile class dynamics and dead campus culture
STEM: multiple students independently warn the STEM program is weak and not worth the cost
key_quotes: A Jewish-Israeli student was repeatedly spit on by fellow students and his piano recital flier was vandalized with antisemitic graffiti, including a swastika and 'Death to the Zionists, Hitler was right'. The student was assigned a protective detail of two FBI agents because the university was unable to ensure their safety
I went to a somewhat poor high school and now I am at a private university with a bunch of rich white snobby kids WHO DONT EVEN LIKE TO PARTY! I really just hate the social scene here
I want to study STEM, and I've heard that AU stem program is weak

key_points: Lincoln Scholars program offers rare political philosophy intellectual community
SIS is genuinely respected for IR at both undergrad and grad level
DC itself acts as an extended campus — free museums, government access, networking
